attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic accompanied by his wife with a six-month history of tremor in his hands that disappears when he uses his hands for writing or handling utensils. His wife reports that he often stares and does not show as much emotion as before. He still participates in his usual activities and enjoys gardening with his wife. His appetite and sleep habits have remained unchanged. His weight is similar to his last clinic visit six months ago. His wife notices that he is slower in moving than before but walks steadily. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, respiratory rate 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. There is a mild resting tremor and a fixed expression on his face. There is resistance on passive range of motion with his upper extremities. His gait is slow, without any imbalance or falling and his arm does not swing when walking. He scores 26/30 on Mini-Mental Status Examination.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
The patient presents with symptoms consistent with Parkinson's disease, including bradykinesia, resting tremor, rigidity, and hypomimia. In Parkinson's disease, there is a deficiency of dopamine in the brain. The most appropriate next step in management would be to commence carbidopa-levodopa, which is converted into dopamine to compensate for this deficiency. Carbidopa is added to levodopa to enhance its effectiveness by preventing its breakdown in the bloodstream before it reaches the brain. Carbidopa-levodopa helps alleviate the motor symptoms of Parkinson's disease and remains the gold standard treatment.

In comparison to dopamine agonists, levodopa has been shown to have improved benefit for motor symptoms, with notable higher risk for dyskinesias. Dopamine agonists also have increased rates of side effects such as nausea, orthostatic hypotension and impulse control. Levodopa also has shown to have small advantage over monoamine oxidase B inhibitors in regards to mobility, with an improved side effect profile.

Rotigotine is a dopamine agonist available as a transdermal patch. While it can be used as initial therapy, particularly in younger patients (under 60 years) to delay levodopa-related motor complications, it is generally less effective than levodopa for motor symptom control in older patients.

Amantadine acts on various neurotransmitter pathways has modest antiparkinsonian effects and is primarily used to treat levodopa-induced dyskinesias rather than as initial therapy. It would not be the most appropriate choice for this patient's presenting symptoms.

Selegiline is a monoamine oxidase B (MAO-B) inhibitor that can be used as monotherapy in early Parkinson's disease. However, its effects are modest compared to levodopa, and it would not be the best choice for initial therapy in this 62-year-old patient with established motor symptoms.

Rasagiline is another MAO-B inhibitor with similar properties to selegiline. While it may have some neuroprotective effects, its symptomatic benefit is limited compared to levodopa, making it a less appropriate choice for initial therapy in this case.
learningPoints: Parkinson's disease is a neurodegenerative disorder that mostly presents in later life with generalized slowing of movements (bradykinesia) and at least one other symptom of resting tremor or rigidity.
The mainstay of current Parkinson's disease treatment are levodopa-based preparations, designed to replace the dopamine in the depleted striatum.
Dopamine agonists may be considered as initial therapy in younger patients to delay levodopa-related motor complications.
references: Bloem BR, Okun MS, Klein C. Parkinson's disease. The Lancet. 2021 Jun 12;397(10291):2284-303. Available online at ; https://repository.ubn.ru.nl/bitstream/handle/2066/235670/235670.pdf

Armstrong MJ, Okun MS. Diagnosis and treatment of Parkinson disease: a review. Jama. 2020 Feb 11;323(6):548-60. Available online at ; https://opal.ils.unc.edu/~jiaerji/project2/images/part3/Diagnosis-and-Treatment-of-Parkinson%20Disease.pdf

PD Med Collaborative Group, Gray R, Ives N, et al. Long-term effectiveness of dopamine agonists and monoamine oxidase B inhibitors compared with levodopa as initial treatment for Parkinson's disease (PD MED): a large, open-label, pragmatic randomised trial [published correction appears in Lancet. 2014 Sep 27;384(9949):1186]. Lancet. 2014;384(9949):1196-1205. doi:10.1016/S0140-6736(14)60683-8. Available online at ; https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lancet/article/PIIS0140-6736(14)60683-8/fulltext
